title: Study in Finland: A Starter Guide for International Students
content:
Finland is consistently ranked among the top countries for higher education quality, research output, and student experience. For international students considering a master's degree, Finland offers a growing number of English-taught programmes, particularly in technology, computer science, and engineering.

## University System

Finland has two types of higher education institutions: universities and universities of applied sciences (UAS). Universities focus on academic research and theory. UAS institutions offer more professionally oriented degrees. For master's programmes aimed at international research-track students, universities are the typical choice.

The major research universities in Finland include the University of Helsinki, Aalto University, University of Tampere (now Tampere University), University of Oulu, University of Jyväskylä, University of Turku, LUT University, and the University of Vaasa.

## Language of Instruction

Many Finnish universities offer full master's programmes in English, particularly in STEM fields. Programmes in humanities, law, and education are more commonly taught in Finnish. When browsing programme listings, filter by language of instruction to confirm English availability for each programme.

## Academic Calendar

The Finnish academic year typically runs from September through May. Most master's programmes are two years in duration. Application periods for autumn intake generally open in late autumn or early winter of the preceding year, with deadlines falling in January or February.

## Tuition Fees

Finland introduced tuition fees for non-EU/EEA students in 2017. Fees vary by institution and programme. Common ranges for master's programmes are EUR 8,000 to EUR 18,000 per academic year. EU and EEA citizens are typically exempt from tuition fees. Always confirm the current fee for your specific programme directly on the university's official website, as amounts change.

## Scholarships and Funding

The Finnish government does not operate a general scholarship programme for international master's students. Some Finnish universities offer merit-based fee waivers or scholarships for incoming non-EU/EEA students. These are university-specific and competitive. Check the financial support section of each university's admissions pages for current availability.

## Student Life

Finland offers a high standard of living, strong public services, and a generally safe environment. Cities like Helsinki, Tampere, and Oulu have active international student communities. Student unions play an important role in campus life and provide access to subsidised housing, healthcare, and social activities.

## How to Apply

Most Finnish universities accept applications through their own online portals or through the national Studyinfo.fi platform. Application requirements typically include academic transcripts, a motivation letter, CV, and proof of English language proficiency. Some programmes require additional documents such as a portfolio or references.

For detailed and current information on Finnish higher education, the Study in Finland website operated by the Finnish National Agency for Education is the primary official source.

title: How to Compare English-Taught Master's Programmes in Finland
content:
Choosing between English-taught master's programmes in Finland requires looking beyond rankings and considering factors that affect your day-to-day study experience and post-graduation outcomes. This guide outlines the key dimensions to compare when evaluating programmes.

## Programme Scope and Specialisation

Finnish universities often bundle related specialisations under one programme umbrella. For example, Aalto's Computer, Communication and Information Sciences programme contains distinct majors such as Computer Science and Machine Learning, Data Science and Artificial Intelligence. Similarly, Tampere University's Computing Sciences programme splits into specialisations like Data Science and Software, Systems and Internet.

When comparing programmes, look past the top-level programme name and examine the available specialisations. Confirm which specialisation is available in English and which courses are offered in that track.

## Research vs. Coursework Orientation

Some programmes are designed primarily for students intending to continue into doctoral research. Others are oriented toward industry roles. Review the programme's thesis requirements, research group affiliations, and alumni outcomes if that information is available. University websites typically describe the programme's orientation and career profiles.

## Tuition Fees and Financial Support

For non-EU/EEA students, tuition fees are a significant variable. Fees across Finnish master's programmes range from roughly EUR 8,000 to EUR 18,000 per year. Some universities charge different amounts per faculty or programme. Always check the fee for the specific programme, not just the university average.

If a university offers a scholarship or tuition waiver, confirm the eligibility criteria, the application process, and whether it requires a separate application or is automatic at admission.

## Admission Requirements

Typical requirements include a bachelor's degree in a relevant field, English language test scores (IELTS or TOEFL), a motivation letter, and sometimes a CV or references. Some programmes, particularly in computer science, may specify minimum quantitative or programming backgrounds.

Admission competitiveness varies. Top programmes at Aalto and the University of Helsinki receive high application volumes. Programmes at regional universities may be more accessible with equivalent qualifications.

## Location and Cost of Living

Helsinki is the most expensive city in Finland. Tampere, Oulu, Jyväskylä, Turku, and Vaasa offer lower costs of living while still providing strong academic environments. For students on a budget, a programme in a smaller city can meaningfully reduce total study costs even if tuition fees are comparable.

Consider proximity to relevant industries. Helsinki and Espoo (where Aalto's main campus is) concentrate the largest technology employers. Tampere has a strong industrial and technology sector. Oulu has historically been linked to telecommunications.

## How to Read a Programme Page

When reviewing an official programme page, look for:

- Confirmed language of instruction (not just programme-level but course-level)
- Current tuition fee for non-EU/EEA students
- Application deadline for the next intake cycle
- Available specialisations or majors within the programme
- Thesis requirements and type
- Any specific admission prerequisites beyond the standard

If the page does not clearly state the language of instruction, contact the admissions office before applying.

## Using DegreeWiki

DegreeWiki lists master's programmes with verified details from official sources. Use programme filters to narrow by country, field, language, and other criteria. Each programme page links to the official university source so you can confirm current information directly.
